q: The environment is the surroundings comprising of hydrosphere, lithosphere, atmosphere and biosphere.
The pathways that allow different chemical substances to move through living and non-living forms and their locations in the biosphere are called a biogeochemical cycle.
The cycling of elements is interconnected with the water cycle.
Mineral nutrients are cycled, either rapidly or slowly, through the entire biosphere and from one living organism to another.
Most of the Earth’s carbon is stored in the atmosphere.
The rest of the Earth's carbon is located in the ocean, atmosphere, and in living organisms.
Plants absorb carbon dioxide during photosynthesis.
Plants release carbon dioxide when they decay.
Respiration is the process of gas exchange between the atmosphere and living organisms.
In the fossilisation process, the animal and plant residues become sediment and eventually turn into rock and minerals.
The carbon is stored as coal and other fossil fuels in rocks and other geological deposits.
The combustion of fossil fuels and organic materials gives off carbon dioxide, water, and energy.

The nitrogen cycle is a biogeochemical process through which nitrogen is converted into many forms, consecutively passing from the atmosphere to the soil and then to organisms, and back into the atmosphere.
The nitrogen cycle involves the following steps: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, nitrogen assimilation, ammonification, and denitrification.
Unlike many other cycling elements, phosphorus can be found in the air.
Phosphorus is mainly cycling through water, soil and sediments.
Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plants and animals in the form of ions $PO_{4}^{3-}$ and $HPO_{4}^{2-}$.
Phosphorus is also a building block of certain parts of the human including DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and animal bodies, such as bones and teeth.
Phosphate salts that are released from rocks through weathering usually dissolve in water present in soil and will be absorbed by plants.
The phosphorus cycle moves through plants and animals much faster than it does through rocks and sediments.
Marine birds play a unique role in the phosphorus cycle, as they take phosphorus-containing fish out of the ocean and return to land, where they defecate.
Guano contains a high level of phosphorus and in this way, marine birds return phosphorus from the ocean to the land.
Sulphur is one of the components that make up proteins and vitamins.
Animals absorb sulphur when sulphur-containing compounds are dissolved in water.
Most of the Earth's sulphur is found in rocks and salts or buried deep in the ocean in oceanic sediments.
Natural sources of sulphur in the atmosphere include volcanic eruptions, bacterial processes, or decaying organisms.
When sulphur dioxide enters the atmosphere, it will react with oxygen to produce sulphur trioxide gas ($SO_{3}$), or with other chemicals in the atmosphere, to produce sulphur salts.
Sulphuric acid may also be produced from dimethyl sulphide emitted to the atmosphere by plankton species.
Ocean acidification refers to a reduction in the pH of the ocean over an extended period of time, caused primarily by the uptake of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
Ocean acidification interferes with the ability of marine organisms (including corals, Dungeness crabs, and snails) to build their shells and skeletons.
Excess nitrogen and phosphorus that enter the ecosystem from fertiliser runoff and from sewage cause excessive growth of algae.
Algal blooms block light and therefore kill aquatic plants in rivers, lakes, and seas.
The subsequent death and decay of aquatic plants deplete dissolved oxygen, which leads to the death of aquatic organisms.
The extraction of metals such as copper, lead, and zinc from sulphur-containing ores releases sulphur dioxide.
Acid rain damages the natural environment by lowering the pH of lakes, thus killing many of the resident plants and aquatic animals.
Environmental pollution is caused by the harmful disposal of substances into the environment.
Heavy metals are metals with high density and toxic effects even at very low concentrations.
Pure arsenic is rarely found in nature and is not very toxic, but it can be oxidised easily in humid air and becomes toxic by conversion to arsenic anhydride.
Arsenic is mostly found in the form of +3 and +5 oxidation states but is not in elemental form in nature.
Compounds such as calcium arsenide, lead arsenide and sodium arsenate are used for the production of paint, glass, ceramics, and semiconductors.
Sodium arsenate is used to kill some parasites in fruit cultivation and potato growing.
Cadmium is released into the atmosphere predominantly as elemental cadmium and cadmium oxide from coal combustion or cadmium chloride from waste incineration.
Cadmium exists as the +2 oxidation state in nature.
Tobacco smoking is also a significant source of cadmium exposure.
Lead and its derivatives are industrial toxic substances that have been widely used by humans for centuries.
Lead enters the atmosphere largely in the exhaust fumes from internal combustion engines.
The primary use of lead is in the manufacture of batteries, and in the production of paint, alloys and metal products in the industry.
Elemental mercury occurs in nature.
Inorganic mercury can be transformed into methylmercury by microorganisms such as phytoplanktons and fungi.
Methylmercury bioaccumulates in fish and shellfish.
Mercury is generally taken into the body in the form of vapour.
A pesticide is a substance that kills a pest, or it prevents or reduces the damage a pest may cause.
Pesticides are often grouped into "families" because they act on the pest in the same way.
Adsorption of pesticides occurs because of the attraction between a chemical and soil particles.
Degradation is the process of pesticide breakdown after application.
Persistent organic pollutants (POPs) are carbon-based organic compounds that have the special abilities to be long-lasting and to resist breakdown into safer substances.
POPs are lipophilic in nature.
Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are organic chemicals that can vaporise.
VOCs react with nitrogen oxides emitted mainly from vehicles, power plants and industrial activities to form ozone, which in turn helps the formation of fine particulates.
The accumulation of ozone, fine particulates and other gaseous pollutants results in smog that reduces visibility.
The burning of plastics releases toxic gases like dioxins, furans, mercury and polychlorinated biphenyls into the atmosphere.
Radioactive pollution is the result of released radionuclides in the environment.
A radionuclide is an atom with in unstable nucleus which has excessive energy.
Radioactivity is a spontaneous process of decay or disintegration of heavy nuclei generating different types of radiation such as alpha particles, beta particles and gamma rays.
Alpha particles can be blocked by a few pieces of paper.
Beta particles pass through paper but are stopped by aluminium foil.
Gamma rays are the most difficult to stop and require concrete, lead, or other heavy shielding to block them.
The majority of background radiation occurs naturally from minerals and a small fraction comes from man-made elements.
Background radiation is a measure of the level of ionising radiation naturally present in the environment at a particular location.
Uranium and thorium are radionuclides naturally found on the Earth.
Prolonged exposure to radon gas can cause lung cancer.
Uranium-235, americium-241, and plutonium-239 are fissile materials produced from a breeder reactor.
Ionisation smoke detectors use americium-241 as a source of alpha particles.
Alpha particles from the americium source ionise air molecules.
A radiotracer is a radioactive isotope used to study the dynamic behaviour of various chemical and biological changes in a system.
Carbon-14 is radiocarbon dating which can be used to date wooden and organic objects, especially in archaeology.
Caesium-137 is used as a tracer in medical radiation therapy devices for treating cancer.
Nuclear reactors use fissile heavy atoms ($^{233}U$, $^{235}U$, $^{239}Pu$) as fuel instead of fossil fuels.
Nuclear bombs are weapons having massive destructive power.
The explosion of a nuclear weapon releases a combination of heat, blast waves and radiation.
Radionuclides found in water include potassium-40, radium-226 and radium-228.
Radioactive materials may be accumulated in blood and certain vital organs, causing tumours and cancer.
Fissile heavy atoms are materials capable of sustaining a nuclear fission chain reaction.
Chemistry plays an essential role to achieve the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
Green chemistry is the design of chemical products and processes that reduce or eliminate the generation of hazardous substances.
Agricultural sustainability is the area that requires green chemistry strategies due to their direct impact on human and environmental health.
Organic farming uses fertilisers of organic origin such as compost manure, green manure, and bone meal instead of chemical fertilisers.
Plant-based pesticides that have minimum risks include essential oils and insecticidal soaps.
Plant-based insecticidal soaps are toxic to animals and birds.
Neem leaves consist of insecticidal ingredients and they can be used as an insecticide.
A plant-based pesticide forms a stable water soluble complex with nutrient molecules to prevent mineralisation in the soil.
Municipal and non-hazardous industrial wastes have been converted to energy by direct burning in a process known as waste gasification or pyrolysis.
In the waste gasification method, organic content produces a mixture of hydrogen and carbon monoxide.
Solar, wind, hydroelectric, biomass, biorefineries, geothermal, and tidal energy are important resources that will replace carbon-containing sources (fossil fuels).
Ethanol is the most important alcohol which can be produced by converting the sugar content of any starch material into alcohol with the evolution of carbon dioxide ($CO_{2}$).
Biodiesel is a form of diesel fuel derived from plants or animals and consisting of long-chain fatty acid esters.
Biodiesel oil is synthesised from plant oils by removing the by-product glycerine molecule.
Glycerine is a valuable raw material for soap production.
The combustion of fossil fuel generates sulphur compounds whereas biodiesel does not.

The main purpose of wastewater treatment is to remove the various contaminants that are present in the wastewater namely suspended solids, organic carbon, nutrients, inorganic salts, heavy metals and pathogens.
Photocatalysts binary metal oxides nanomaterials (e.g., $TiO_{2}$, $ZnO$, $Fe_{2}O_{3}$) are used to mineralise, decompose, and oxidise the pollutants from wastewater.
Tetraethyllead (TEL) was used as an anti-knocking agent.
Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) is now used as an additive for unleaded petrol to replace tetraethyllead.
Bioplastics are derived from renewable biomass sources such as corn starch, vegetable oils and fats.
Solvents like tetrachloroethene ($Cl_{2}C=CCl_{2}$) used in dry cleaning of clothes, pollute the groundwater and are carcinogenic.
In the liquid $CO_{2}$ cleaning process, dry cleaners pressurise and then liquefy $CO_{2}$ gas to make it a powerful solvent for cleaning clothing items.
Large amounts of adipic acid, $HOOC(CH_{2})_{4}COOH$, are used each year for the production of nylon, polyurethanes, lubricants and plasticisers.
Benzene is a standard substrate for the production of adipic acid.
Glucose can be converted into adipic acid by an enzyme discovered in genetically modified bacteria.
Hexavalent chromium plating is most commonly known as chrome plating.
Hexavalent chromium itself is a hazardous substance and carcinogen.
Trivalent chromium is considered as an environmentally friendly alternative to hexavalent chromium.
Trivalent chromium plating uses chromium sulphate or chromium chloride as its main ingredient.
Trivalent chromium is less toxic than hexavalent chromium.
The 7 R's of a green environment for sustainable development are Rethink, Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Regift, and Recycle.
Green chemistry is also called sustainable chemistry.
